coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot as electron acceptor anchored on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet for improving rhodamine B degradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jourshabani, Milad
Long, Nguyen Van Duc
Asrami, Mahdieh Razi
Pho, Quoc Hue
Lee, Byeong-Kyu
Hessel, Volker
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold significant promise for wastewater treatment because of their remarkable attributes, including robust stability, outstanding biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, superior water dispersibility, cost-effective production, and exceptional photo-stability. In this study, CQDs were synthesized using microplasma and tested for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) (5 mg/L) under different conditions, with a Solar simulator and UVA illumination. Specifically, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (NCN) in conjugation with carbon quantum dots (NCN@CQD) were prepared with different amounts of CQD. The synthesized CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD underwent characterization using various compositional and structural analytical techniques. The results show that nitrogen species were effectively incorporated into the CQDs framework and CQDs are anchored on NCN forming NCN@CQD successfully. Subsequently, all samples were then investigated in degrading Rhodamin B, RhB, to assess the photocatalytic activity. The results reveal that the presence of CQDs substantially improved the photocatalytic efficiency of NCN. Specifically, the CQDs anchored on NCN exhibited a substantial enhancement in the photoactivity for RhB decomposition under a solar light simulator, surpassing that of pure NCN. The amount of CQD in the NCN@CQD sample exerted a strong influence on the photocatalytic activity. All NCN@CQD samples exhibited remarkable enhancements in both adsorption and photocatalytic performance compared to pure CQD and NCN samples. In an optimization study, a best NCN@CQ photocatalyst was identified, showing a 40 % enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency compared to the use of NCN alone. A visible light photocatalytic activity of 91 % can be attained within 150 min, exhibiting a reaction rate constant of 0.0135 min−1 toward RhB decomposition, which was double as for the individual NCN (0.0067 min−1). The boosted photocatalytic activity resulting from the synergistic effect between NCNs and CQDs can be attributed to the efficient function of CQD as an electron acceptor. The proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB is illustrated in detail.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dot
Graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Photocatalytic wastewater treatment
rhodamine B

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (NCN)
Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dot
Preparation of CQD anchored on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet (NCN@CQD)
N-doped Carbon quantum dots anchored on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the NCQD synthesis process using FA through the application of the kINPen®IND microplasma system.).
Photodegradation of RhB under (a) solar simulator and (b) UVA illumination.
SEM images of the prepared (a) and (b) CQD; (c) and (d) NCN; (e) and (f) NCN@CQD(2).
HRTEM images of the prepared NCN@CQD(2) at (a) 30 kX; (b) 50 kX; and (c) 150 kX.
The XRD patterns of the prepared CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD(2).
Representative FTIR spectrum of the prepared (a) CQD; (b) NCN; (c) NCN@CQD(2); comparison of NCN and NCN@CQD(2) (d).
(a) Adsorption equilibrium in the dark condition and (b) photocatalytic degradation of RhB in aqueous solution over CQD under solar simulator.
Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in aqueous solution over CQD under UVA illumination.
Pictures of NCN@CQD(x) before and after light illumination compared to pure NCN.
Time-dependent UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) NCN; (b) NCN@CQD(1); (c) NCN@CQD(2); and (d) NCN@CQD(3).

(a) Time-dependent UV–Vis absorption spectra over all samples; (b) graph illustrating the relationship of −ln(C/C0) against the reaction time for the degradation of RhB employing various photocatalysts.
The photographs of the reactor at t = 0 and t = 150 min under Solar simulator.
Schematic illustration of the possible photodegradation of RhB on the NCN@CQD composite under visible light.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot as electron acceptor anchored on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet for improving rhodamine B degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan 44610, Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan 44610, Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, University of Warwick, Coventry, CV4 7AL, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Adelaide, Adelaide 5005, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Adelaide, Adelaide 5005, Australia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold significant promise for wastewater treatment because of their remarkable attributes, including robust stability, outstanding biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, superior water dispersibility, cost-effective production, and exceptional photo-stability.

In this study, CQDs were synthesized using microplasma and tested for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) (5 mg/L) under different conditions, with a Solar simulator and UVA illumination. Specifically, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (NCN) in conjugation with carbon quantum dots (NCN@CQD) were prepared with different amounts of CQD. The synthesized CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD underwent characterization using various compositional and structural analytical techniques. The results show that nitrogen species were effectively incorporated into the CQDs framework and CQDs are anchored on NCN forming NCN@CQD successfully. Subsequently, all samples were then investigated in degrading Rhodamin B, RhB, to assess the photocatalytic activity. The results reveal that the presence of CQDs substantially improved the photocatalytic efficiency of NCN. Specifically, the CQDs anchored on NCN exhibited a substantial enhancement in the photoactivity for RhB decomposition under a solar light simulator, surpassing that of pure NCN. The amount of CQD in the NCN@CQD sample exerted a strong influence on the photocatalytic activity. All NCN@CQD samples exhibited remarkable enhancements in both adsorption and photocatalytic performance compared to pure CQD and NCN samples. In an optimization study, a best NCN@CQ photocatalyst was identified, showing a 40 % enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency compared to the use of NCN alone. A visible light photocatalytic activity of 91 % can be attained within 150 min, exhibiting a reaction rate constant of 0.0135 min−1 toward RhB decomposition, which was double as for the individual NCN (0.0067 min−1). The boosted photocatalytic activity resulting from the synergistic effect between NCNs and CQDs can be attributed to the efficient function of CQD as an electron acceptor. The proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB is illustrated in detail.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, rapid population growth and industrialization have caused widespread water pollution in a variety of sources, including surface water, groundwater, wastewater, and drinking water. This pollution includes diverse pollutants such as organic compounds, heavy metals, inorganic substances and complex compounds [40]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop wastewater reclamation methods that are advanced, environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and highly efficient. Numerous technologies have been employed to decontaminate wastewater, with key methods encompassing membrane filtration [61], electrodialysis [2], adsorption [56], precipitation [5], and electrochemical reduction [34]. Nevertheless, these methods frequently require significant energy and can introduce complexity by transferring contaminants between various fluids and wastes, leading to the formation of by-products during the wastewater treatment process. Considering the economic and social development aspects, it's crucial to explore mild reaction conditions and efficient catalysts for pollutant removal in wastewater. This approach aims to minimize energy requirements and simplify the treatment process, making it more economically viable [41].

Among various available technologies, photocatalysis stands out as a promising solution for wastewater treatment [33]. It offers mild operating conditions, a straightforward process, and environmentally friendly attributes. Photocatalysis offers an effective means to break down organic pollutants in wastewater into water and/or other small molecules. Additionally, it proves efficient in reducing or oxidizing inorganic pollutants, converting them into harmless substances [24]. The simplicity, efficiency, and green characteristics of photocatalysis are favorable process characteristics.

Graphitic carbon nitride, a recently discovered material exhibiting properties akin to graphene, has garnered interest as an active photo(electro)catalyst responsive to visible light in diverse applications [10,12,31]. These applications encompass the breakdown of organic pollutants via photocatalysis, the transformation of carbon dioxide into hydrocarbons, and the production of hydrogen and oxygen [19;27]. Over the past decade, it has become evident that graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (NCNs), as depicted in Fig. 1 , hold the most promise as metal-free materials for driving solar-driven chemical reactions. NCN possesses a suitable bandgap of 2.7 eV, enabling it to be efficiently excited by visible light [49]. NCN is characterised by its cost-effectiveness, abundance, and eco-friendliness, meeting the demands of applications in environmental protection and energy conservation [57]. However, further improvements are still necessary to enhance the photocatalytic performance of pure NCN. Researchers continue to explore methods and techniques to optimize its capabilities in order to achieve even better results in the field of photocatalysis.

Numerous studies have been performed to improve the photocatalytic capabilities of NCN [9,14;22]. One approach that has shown promise is the creation of junctions between NCN and other semiconductors or metal nanoparticles. The goal of this method is to separate electron-hole pairs created by light and prevent their recombination [30;54]. Recent investigations have focused on loading noble metal nanoparticles, such as Pt [35], Pd [18], and Ag [13], onto the surface of NCN nanosheets. This technique has proven to be a successful means of improving the photocatalytic performance of NCN. Noble metals serve as efficient electron acceptors [6], effectively reducing the recombination rate of electron and hole pairs within NCN nanosheets and improving their absorption of visible light [30].

Carbon quantum dots represent an emerging category of nanomaterials characterized by their fluorescence attributes. In comparison to traditional fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots possess several unique advantages. Firstly, they exhibit excellent biocompatibility [36], making them suitable for various biological applications. Additionally, carbon quantum dots demonstrate good solubility [51], low toxicity [55], remarkable photostability [28], and have a small size [7]. However, the limited yield of carbon quantum dots [45] and the scarcity of functional groups on their surface restrict their fluorescence performance and applications (Liping [26]. To address these limitations, heteroatom doping has emerged as one of the most effective approaches to modify the bulk/size, surface, and morphology of carbon quantum dots [46]. This technique leads to improvements in their spectral properties and broadens their potential applications. In particular, the introduction of amine (–NH2) functional groups onto the surface of carbon quantum dots has been found to enhance their photoluminescence [32]. The introduction of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has demonstrated as an effective approach for overcoming the typically low quantum yields observed in traditional carbon quantum dots. CQD allows for the creation of different emission centers and traps within the CQDs, leading to enhanced fluorescence performance. Furthermore, it enables modifications of the electronic structures (Liangliang [25].

Out of various methods used in the manufacturing of CQDs, including laser ablation, hydrothermal, pyrolysis, and ultrasonic processing, plasma synthesis has demonstrated the highest energy efficiency [37]. However, it lacks mass efficiency compared to other processes typically conducted in larger-scale reactors. With a plasma synthesis method using a plasma jet, the issue of low productivity was addressed, and the new process demonstrated to enhance mass transport in an intensified gas–liquid regime. This process also enables catalysis on metal particles within the liquid microvolume, enabling the synthesis of grams of material [38].

Despite substantial advancements in the synthesis and application of CQDs, there are limited reports where bare CQDs have been directly utilised as active photocatalytic materials. Most studies have focused on combining CQDs with other semiconductors to enhance their performance [29,42;59].

In this study, the initial step involved the use of microplasma synthesis to produce CQDs. Subsequently, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets in conjunction with CQDs were synthesized, creating a hybrid material referred to as NCN@CQD. Following characterization through various techniques, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared CQD, NCN, and hybrid material was subsequently evaluated and compared. Rhodamine B (RhB) served as the model for an organic pollutant, and the evaluations were performed under various illumination conditions, including Solar simulator and UVA illumination. The study specifically examined the impact of CQD amount on the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized samples. Ultimately, a kinetic model was investigated to discover the reaction rate constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (NCN)
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for preparing NCN was detailed in our prior publication [4]. Cyanuric acid and melamine, each in equimolar quantities (15.8 mmol), were separately dissolved in 80 mL of boiling water until fully dissolved, forming a complex. The solutions were then combined in a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and stirred for one hour. Subsequently, the autoclave placed in an oven at 100 °C for 4 h before cooling naturally. The resulting suspension was centrifuged, washed with distilled water, and the remaining precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 24 h, yielding a white powder for further processing. This powder was subjected to calcination at 540 °C in a furnace for 4 h under a nitrogen environment. The resulting material was then transferred to a covered crucible and calcined at 540 °C in a furnace for 3 h under an air atmosphere, resulting in the formation of NCN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: In our previous studies [39,38], microplasma synthesis was employed for the production of CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The CQDs were synthesized using a kINPen®IND-induced microplasma in an open reactor, which had dimensions of 15 mm x 9 mm (width x height). The microplasma jet, supplied by Neoplas GmbH, Germany, was linked to a 110 VAC power source (50 Hz) and measured 105 x 180 x 330 mm (H x W x D). A continuous flow of argon gas (99.9 %) was introduced into the jet for generating a microplasma. Folic acid (FA) was dissolved in a 0.5 M NaHCO3 solvent to create a homogeneous orange solution. This FA solution was then fed to an open reactor with 0.5 g of metal flakes (500–1000 μm), amplifying the electrical field and inducing plasma-catalytic effects to enhance the photoluminescence of CQDs, resulting in a dark brown solution.

For the isolation of CQDs, the treated solution was precipitated with 0.5 M HCl before undergoing centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the unreacted FA pellet. The supernatant was dialyzed in water (1000 Da dialysis bag) for three days, yielding pure CQDs dried with a freeze-dryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD anchored on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet (NCN@CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: Various quantities of CQDs (0.0, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mg) were individually dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water containing 0.2 g of NCN, ensuring a uniform mixture through ultrasonication. After overnight stirring at 80 °C, each sample was collected, and the powder was dried at 80 °C in an oven. The resulting powder was labeled as NCN@CQD(x), where x represents the amount of CQD added (1.0, 2.0, or 3.0 mg).

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed to assess the crystalline structure of CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD. The assessments were carried out using a Rigaku MiniFlex 600 XRD instrument, utilizing Cu-Kα1 radiation with a wavelength of 1.54056 Å. Sample scanning was conducted within the range of 3 to 80 degrees at a rate of 0.2 degrees per minute.

The morphology of the synthesized samples was investigated through field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) utilizing an FEI Nova NanoSEM equipped with a Schottky-type thermal FE gun, operating at 15 kV. Additionally, a JEOL JEM-2100F, operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, was utilized for the performance of high-resolution transmission electron spectroscopy (HRTEM).

The Varian Model-670/620 spectrometer was employed to analyze Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) in the range of 4,000 to 400 cm-1. These spectra were utilized to characterize the functional groups present on the surfaces of CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from SPECS Sage XPS was a technique for analysing the surface chemistry of NCN@CQD.

RhB finds extensive applications in dyeing paints, acrylics, various fabrics, and biological products. When released directly into water, it poses a significant threat to organisms due to its high toxicity [53]. Given its widespread use and known environmental impact, RhB has been chosen as the target pollutant in numerous studies [11;23;47;53], including our own. In particular, the photocatalytic performance of CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD were evaluated for the degradation of RhB using a Solar light simulator (Fig. 3 a). The light intensity was set at a fixed value of 100 mW cm−2, which closely matched the Air Mass 1.5 Global (AM 1.5 G) solar spectrum’s intensity. The experiment was conducted in a specially designed cylindrical Pyrex-glass reactor containing the target pollutant with an initial concentration of 5 mg/L, along with 20 mg of the respective photocatalyst. To maintain the reaction at room temperature, circulating water was used for cooling. The total reaction mixture volume was 100 mL, and the Solar light simulator was positioned at the top of the reactor. Before the photocatalytic reaction commenced, all experiments were conducted in dark conditions for 1 h to allow the pollutant molecules to adsorb onto the surfaces of CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD to achieve adsorption equilibrium. At specific time intervals, approximately 2.5 mL of the sample was extracted from the reactor, and the concentration of RhB was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at λmax = 553 nm. To facilitate quantitative calculations and analysis, a calibration curve was generated.

We also examined the photocatalytic performance of the CQD sample under UVA illumination using a 20-watt Black-type Fluorescent Lamp (Fig. 3b). To achieve artificial irradiation, the Fluorescent Lamp was positioned at the top of the batch reactor, maintaining a constant distance of 5 cm between the lamp and the reactor for all measurements. For each experimental run, 20 mg of the CQD sample was dispersed in 100 mL of water. RhB at aconcentration of 5 mg/L and the photocatalyst were introduced into the reactor. The mixture was stirred for 60 min to allow for adsorption equilibrium in the absence of light. The photocatalytic degradation reaction was initiated by activating the light source.

The morphology and structure of the fabricated samples, namely CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD(2), were assessed using SEM, as depicted in Fig. 4 . It's worth noting, as mentioned earlier, that the resulting powder was labeled as NCN@CQD(x), where x corresponds to the quantity of CQD introduced (1.0, 2.0, or 3.0 mg). The SEM image reveals the nearly nanorod-shaped morphology of the CQDs. The NCN structure exhibits partially curly edges, attributed to its ultrathin composition, strategically designed to reduce surface tension. This characteristic is consistent with the construction of exfoliated carbon scrolls through graphite peeling. Prior to CQD modification, the surface of NCN sheets appeared relatively smooth. In contrast, after modification, numerous CQDs were evenly dispersed across the NCN surface in the NCN@CQD(2) sample. Notably, the scrolled morphology of NCN@CQD(2) remains preserved.

To delve deeper into the morphology of the composite photocatalysts, NCN@CQD, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis was employed. Fig. 5 depicts HRTEM images of the prepared NCN@CQD(2) sample at (a) 30 kX, (b) 50 kX, and (c) 150 kX magnifications. The HRTEM images show that no lattice spacing associated with CQDs could be observed, possibly due to the fact that the carbon nitride governed the NCN@CQD(2) composite structure and the particle size of CQDs is quite small. In our previous work, CQD with a particle size of 3.13 ± 0.65 nm was identified using HRTEM analysis [39].

Fig. 6 shows the XRD patterns of the CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD(2) photocatalysts. The XRD pattern of CQD reveals a broad peak at 25.5°, signifying the decomposition of folic acid's crystalline structure into crystalline carbon dots through microplasma. This broad peak corresponds to the (002) surface, consistent with previous findings [39]. These results confirm the highly crystalline nature of the carbon dots, suggesting the presence of oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups with multi-layered structures in the core. Certain sharp peaks are attributed to NaCl and NaHCO3 crystals originating from the solvent media.

In the case of pure NCN, the most prominent diffraction peak appears at 27.5 degrees, attributed to the characteristic (002) interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic layers. Additionally, distinct diffraction peaks at 12.8°, 17.7°, and 21.6° indicates the in-plane structural packing motif [17]. As displayed in Fig. 6, no obvious diffraction peaks of CQD were found in the sample of NCN@CQD(2), illustrating a low amount of CQD compared with NCN. The XRD results are entirely consistent with HRTEM images.

In our prior publication [39], carbon quantum dots (CQD) with a particle size of 3.13 ± 0.65 nm were identified through HRTEM analysis. In comparison, NCN consists of elongated nanosheets, making it an ideal substrate. The overlapping NCN peak observed at 27.5 degrees, coupled with the absence of distinct peaks associated with CQD, may suggest a high level of distribution. This perspective aligns with findings frequently reported in the literature [3,43].

FTIR spectra were employed to analyze the composition of CQD, NCN, and NCN@CQD(2), illustrated in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a displays the characteristic FTIR spectrum of synthesized CQDs, with prominent peaks at 3432 cm−1 attributed to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, while absorption bands at 2969 cm−1 are associated with C–H stretching vibrations [60]. Comparatively, the CQDs spectrum exhibits distinctive bands within the range of 1210 cm−1 to 1680 cm−1, corresponding to CN bond stretching vibrations [52]. Additionally, N–H bending vibrations are observed between 700 cm−1 and 830 cm−1. Notably, peaks at 1608 cm−1, 1508 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, and 1336 cm−1 are associated with characteristic modes of stretching of N–C = O, C–N–C, N–(C)3, and C–N–C [52], while C–H vibrations are evident at 1130 cm−1 [60]. These absorption bands not only confirm the effective nitrogen doping of the carbon quantum dots but also indicate the presence of numerous functional groups on the surface of the CQDs.

The NCN sample exhibits characteristic peaks of absorption within the 1230 cm−1–1640 cm−1 range, corresponding to the modes of vibrations of NCN materials, in agreement with FTIR analysis for NCN [8,16] (as shown in Fig. 7b). Furthermore, a notable peak of vibration at 807 cm-1 is ascribed to the out-of-plane bending vibrations of heptazine rings within the CN matrix [16]. The broad band observed in the range of 3300 cm-1–3500 cm-1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H bonds, originating from uncondensed amino groups and adsorbed H2O molecules [16]. As shown in Fig. 7c, the FTIR spectrum of NCN@CQD(2) closely resembles that of NCN. Notably, the shape of the broad band in the 3300 cm−1–3500 cm−1 region in NCN@CQD(2) is the result of a synergistic effect between the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H bonds in NCN and the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations present in CQD (Fig. 7d).

Additionally, XPS analysis was conducted (Figure S1, Supplementary Information Supplementary Information). It was found that oxygen percentage (at.%) is 5.65 % which is much higher than the oxygen content in the bare NCN owing to the adsorbed water [15]. This result suggests the integration of CQD within NCN increases the oxygen percentage as high as 5.65 %. Besides, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s can be resolved into three components centered at 532.3, 534.8, and 536.7 eV, representing the presence of the C–O, O–C = O and C–O–H groups in the CQD [50].

The recombination of electron and hole pairs is a significant challenge for photocatalyst materials, as it diminishes their overall performance of photocatalyst. To address this issue, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer promising potential as they excel in capturing and transferring electrons, exhibiting properties akin to conventional semiconductors [42]. To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples, Rhodamine B (RhB) was selected as a model organic pollutant. In particular, in this study, we carefully evaluated the as-prepared CQD for the degradation of rhodamine B (5 mg/L) under different conditions, with Solar simulator and also UVA illumination, to compare with the performances of the proposed material. To evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the CQD samples, the degradation of RhB was assessed indirectly by measuring the changes in the intensity of the 553 nm absorption peak of the RhB solution. The results show that the prepared CQD material does not exhibit significant photocatalytic activity when exposed to solar simulator and UVA for 4 h as seen in Figs. 8 and 9
, respectively. This means that the CQD material did not demonstrate noticeable efficacy in removing RhB.

Generally, carbon dots (C-dots) may possess the ability to absorb light due to the π–π* transition of sp2 C = C double bonds present in the inner core and various functional groups on the outer layer (–COOH, –OH, –NH2, etc.) [1]. Although in the literature, C-dots are sometimes referred to as quantum dots, they often do not exhibit characteristics resembling typical semiconductor quantum dots (SQD). In this project, we assessed the semiconductor properties of CQD using a wide range of wavelengths (UVA: λ = 315–400 nm) and a Solar simulator (λ ≥ 400 nm), hypothesizing that they could behave as SQDs. It was determined that the present CQD cannot be classified as SCQ, suggesting CQD as Electron Mediators. Subsequently, all experiments focused on the solar simulators, as NCN responded to λ ≥ 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-doped Carbon quantum dots anchored on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: NCN exhibits distinct photophysical and chemical properties, including the ability to harvest light across the visible spectrum, chemical inertness, and a π-π conjugation structure. Moreover, NCN can be synthesized easily and cost-effectively, making it a promising photocatalyst for the degradation of waterborne pollutants. Given these characteristics, NCN is expected to be a suitable match for CQD due to their shared π-π conjugation structure. After successfully synthesizing the graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets in conjunction with N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCN@CQD), their photocatalytic activity was evaluated in this study. Similar to the evaluation of CQD's photocatalytic activity, RhB was also chosen as the model organic pollutant to assess the photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples. The performance of NCN, NCN@CQD(1), NCN@CQD(2), and NCN@CQD(3) was evaluated under Solar simulator, as depicted in Fig. 10 . As can be seen in this Figure, when NCN@CQD is used, the solution gradually shifts from pink to yellow-green, while in the case of pure NCN, the RhB solution retains its dark pink color. This observation indicates that NCN@CQD(1), NCN@CQD(2), and NCN@CQD(3) demonstrate improved RhB degradation compared to pure NCN. Furthermore, the results highlight that the most significant color change occurred when using NCN@CQD(2) for degradation of rhodamine B.

Fig. 11 depicts the significant change in RhB concentration observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCN (Fig. 11a), NCN@CQD(1) (Fig. 11b), NCN@CQD(2) (Fig. 11c), and NCN@CQD(3) (Fig. 11d) over time. After 150 min of exposure to the solar light simulator, the absorption peak of RhB at 553 nm not only significantly diminished but also underwent a shift to approximately 500 nm, signifying the decomposition of RhB's conjugated structure. Additionally, the findings indicate that the absorption reduction was most pronounced when utilizing NCN@CQD(2).

The role of adsorption and photocatalysis was examined separately by stirring the samples in dark conditions for 1 h, followed by 2.5 h of exposure, as shown in Fig. 12 a. The concentration ratio C/C0 was plotted against time to assess the dependence. It is evident that the adsorption process for all samples primarily occurred after 30 min of stirring in the dark, ensuring complete attainment of adsorption equilibrium after 1 h. Fig. 12a demonstrates that all NCN@CQD samples exhibit significantly enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic capabilities compared to pure CQD and NCN samples. Among the NCN@CQD samples, NCN@CQD(2) exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity in RhB decomposition, as evidenced by the steepest slope in the C/C0 curve. Particularly, the order of RhB degradation after utilizing a photocatalyst is as follows: NCN (65 %) < NCN@CQD(1) (79 %) < NCN@CQD(3) (84 %) < NCN@CQD(2) (91 %). The decrease in the photocatalytic activity of the NCN@CQD(3) composite could be attributed to the “shielding effect” of the carbon material, which reduces light absorption and masks the active sites of the catalyst [58].

Put differently, the use of NCN@CQD(1), NCN@CQD(2), and NCN@CQD(3) results in a respective increase of 20 %, 40 %, and 26 % in photocatalytic efficiency compared to using NCN on its own. It is crucial to reiterate that no degradation of RhB was observed under photolysis conditions, further emphasizing the necessity of a photocatalyst for efficient RhB degradation.

The photocatalytic efficiency of NCN is hindered by its high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, despite its absorption in the visible light region [35]. The improved photocatalytic activity observed with the use of NCN@CQD can be attributed to the role of CQD as an effective electron acceptor. In this mechanism, photogenerated electrons on NCN nanosheets are effectively transferred to CQD, thus reducing the recombination rate of electrons and holes [20,44;48]. The presence of CQD significantly enhances the activity through two key factors: (1) inhibiting electron-hole recombination by capturing photoinduced electrons and (2) providing catalytic sites for adsorbed species and reaction intermediates. Fig. 13 showcases photographs of the reactor at t = 0 and t = 150 min, illustrating the effective degradation of RhB in the presence of NCN@CQD(2).

The photocatalytic efficiency was determined using a calibration curve and Equation (1), where η represents the photocatalytic efficiency, C0 denotes the initial concentration, and Ct represents the concentration after t minutes. Additionally, the degradation data exhibited good agreement with the pseudo first-order kinetics model, as described by Equation (2), where kapp (min−1) represents the pseudo first-order reaction rate constant (Fig. 12b). Notably, the corresponding reaction rate constants (k) for NCN, NCN@CQD(1), NCN@CQD(2), and NCN@CQD(3) were found to be 0.0067, 0.0093, 0.013, and 0.0104 min−1, respectively. Note that the evaluation of reaction kinetics involved normalizing and excluding the adsorption part (under dark) from the degradation section. R2 values for linear regression of NCN, NCN/CQD(1), NCN@CQD(2), and NCN@CQD(3) are 0.9871, 0.9837, 0.9985, and 0.9837, respectively. This observation further supports the assertion that the incorporation of CQD with NCN effectively enhances the photocatalytic ability under visible light.
(1)
η =
(
C 0
-
C t
)
C 0
(2)
ln
C 0
C t
=
k
app
× t

Based on the experimental results, the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB was proposed (Fig. 14 ). Accordingly, under visible light irradiation on the NCN@CQD, the electron-hole pairs generate, the produced electrons transfer to the conduction band level to participate in the oxidation reaction and convert the oxygen to •O2 −. Subsequently, the active radicals can attack the RhB and convert them to H2O and CO2. The problem in pure carbon nitride is the fast recombination of generated electrons and holes. In the presence of CQD, the excited electrons of NCN@CQD can transferred to CQD sites, which facilitate the efficient separation of e−/h+ [21;58]. As a result, the generated charge carrier in NCN@CQD will be more active than the pure carbon nitride which can be the reason for the higher photocatalytic activity of NCN@CQD in the degradation of RhB rather than the other samples.

The successful conjugation of graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets with carbon quantum dots resulted in the formation of NCN@CQD. This composite material was subjected to characterization through FT-IR, SEM, HRTEM, XRD, and XPS analyses to ascertain its molecular structure and composition. The findings indicate that the metal-free NCN is a suitable match for CQD, with the hypothesis that CQD can function as an electron acceptor. NCN@CQD proved to be a promising option for degrading RhB, while CQD alone did not exhibit noticeable performance in removing rhodamine B. It could be demonstrated that CQD enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency of NCN, and the extent of RhB degradation depended on the amount of CQD present in the NCN@CQD sample. Particularly, the photocatalytic efficiency can be improved by 20 %, 40 %, and 26 % when utilizing NCN@CQD(1), NCN@CQD(2), and NCN@CQD(3), respectively, in comparison to using NCN alone. Assessing the photocatalytic performances of various NCN@CQD samples, the findings revealed that an optimized variant, NCN@CQD(2), displayed advantageous photocatalytic characteristics with an optimal concentration of CQD. Under visible light, the photocatalytic activity reached 91 % after 150 min, featuring a reaction rate constant of 0.0135 min-1 for RhB decomposition—twice as high compared to the standalone NCN (0.0067 min-1).The enhancement in photocatalysis was ascribed to the heightened formation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, along with the efficient separation of these electrons and holes facilitated by the composite photocatalyst NCN@CQD. The proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB is illustrated in detail.

Milad Jourshabani: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nguyen Van Duc Long: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mahdieh Razi Asrami: Investigation, Data curation. Quoc Hue Pho: Data curation. Byeong-Kyu Lee: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Volker Hessel: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the ERC Grant Surface-COnfined fast modulated Plasma for process and Energy intensification (SCOPE) from the European Commission with the Grant No. 810182. This work was also supported by a Brain Pool (BP) fellowship grant, funded by the Korea government (No. 2019H1D3A1A01070060).
